rationale: The allocation at line 900 is assigned to cfrm->data, where cfrm = &f->cfrm[i] and f = avctx->priv_data. The buffer is stored in the persistent FourXContext.cfrm[] array (long-lived codec private data), not a local pointer. Ownership is transferred to the codec context and the cfrm buffers are released in the codec's close/uninit routine (not in this slice). av_fast_realloc also reuses the existing allocation tracked by cfrm->allocated_size, so no per-call leak occurs. The NeverFree report stems from the analyzer not seeing the close-time cleanup outside the provided slice.

The allocation at line 900 is assigned to cfrm->data, a member of f->cfrm[i] inside FourXContext (avctx->priv_data). This is persistent codec state owned by the AVCodecContext, not a local buffer. It is reused across calls (av_fast_realloc) and freed in the codec's close/uninit routine, not within decode_frame. The NULL is also explicitly checked at line 903 before use, so there is no null-deref. The analyzer flags it as NeverFree because the free occurs outside this function's scope.

The allocation at line 117 is stored into esc->data[0], a field of the decoder's persistent context (avctx->priv_data, EightSvxContext). Ownership is intentionally transferred to the long-lived context struct, which FFmpeg frees in the codec's close callback (eightsvx_decode_close). It is reused across frames (data_idx/data_size tracking) and not leaked within this function. The two-channel partner allocation at 120 already frees data[0] on its own failure, confirming managed ownership.

The allocation at line 120 is stored into esc->data[1], a field of the persistent EightSvxContext (avctx->priv_data). Ownership is transferred to the long-lived context rather than being a local resource. The allocation is guarded so it only happens once (when !esc->data[0]), and the buffer is reused across decode calls and freed in the codec's close callback (eightsvx_decode_close), which is standard FFmpeg lifecycle. The analyzer flags NeverFree because it cannot see the close handler, but this is intentional ownership transfer, not a leak.

The allocation at line 117 is stored into esc->data[0], a field of the persistent EightSvxContext (avctx->priv_data). The buffer is intentionally retained across decode calls (guarded by !esc->data[0]) and is freed in the codec's close callback, not within this frame function. Ownership is transferred to the context, so the absence of a free here is by design.
fix_suggestion: No fix needed; verify eightsvx_decode_close frees esc->data[0] and esc->data[1].

The allocation at line 117 is stored in esc->data[0], a member of the persistent decoder context (avctx->priv_data). The 'NeverFree' finding is intra-procedural and misses the cleanup in the close function, which is outside the provided slice.

The allocation at line 236 is stored into c->mc_meta_charset, where c = avctx->priv_data is the persistent encoder context. Ownership is transferred to the long-lived A64Context struct, which is freed by the codec's close callback (a64multi_close_encoder), not within init. The allocations at lines 236-239 are assigned to fields of the persistent A64Context (avctx->priv_data), transferring ownership to the codec context. FFmpeg frees these in the encoder's close callback (a64multi_close_encoder), which avcodec also invokes when init returns an error. The analyzer flags 'NeverFree' because it only inspects this init function and cannot see the close callback that releases the buffers. This is the standard FFmpeg allocate-in-init/free-in-close ownership pattern, not a leak.

The allocations at lines 236-239 are assigned to fields of the persistent A64Context (avctx->priv_data), not to local variables. On the ENOMEM error path the framework still invokes close to free partially-allocated fields. The slice shows no local leak and no double-free.

The allocation at line 236 is assigned to c->mc_meta_charset, a member of the A64Context held by avctx->priv_data. This is the standard FFmpeg priv_data ownership pattern, so the buffer is not leaked on the normal path. The reported partial-failure path returns ENOMEM but cleanup is handled by the framework-invoked close.

The allocation at line 237 is stored in c->mc_best_cb, a field of the persistent A64Context (avctx->priv_data). Ownership is transferred to the long-lived codec context rather than retained as a local. FFmpeg encoders free these buffers in their close callback (a64multi_close_encoder/AVCodec.close), which the framework also invokes on init failure. This is intentional ownership transfer, not a leak in init scope.

The allocations at lines 236-239 are stored in the long-lived A64Context private data (c->mc_meta_charset, c->mc_best_cb, c->mc_charmap, c->mc_colram). In FFmpeg these per-context buffers are released by the codec's close callback (a64multi_close_encoder via av_freep), not within init. This is intentional ownership transfer to the persistent context, so the analyzer's 'NeverFree' at the allocation site reflects that the free lives in a separate function not included in the slice rather than a genuine leak. The only residual risk is a partial-allocation failure when FF_CODEC_CAP_INIT_CLEANUP is unset, but that is not what the report flags.
fix_suggestion: Confirm a64multi_close_encoder frees all four buffers and that FF_CODEC_CAP_INIT_CLEANUP is set so partial init failures are also cleaned up.

rationale: The allocation at line 107 is assigned to avctx->extradata, a field of the long-lived AVCodecContext. Ownership is transferred to the codec context, which frees extradata during codec teardown (avcodec_close/free_context). The prior av_free(avctx->extradata) at line 106 releases the old buffer before reallocating, and on allocation failure the function returns ENOMEM. There is no leak on any feasible path within this function; the resource is intentionally owned by the persistent struct.

The allocation at line 107 is assigned to avctx->extradata, a field of the long-lived AVCodecContext. Ownership is transferred to the codec context, which frees extradata during codec teardown (avcodec_close/free). The previous buffer is freed at line 106 before reallocation, and the NULL case is handled at lines 108-109. This is not a leak within this function's scope.

av_realloc_array at 1728 reallocates and internally frees the old buffer, but tmp_buf is immediately reassigned to the result (tmp2) at 1734, so the pointer freed by realloc is never freed again. The explicit av_free at 1750 runs only once and only when tmp_buf is a heap pointer (tmp_buf != temp_data). On realloc failure the original pointer is left intact and freed once at 1730-1731 before return. No path frees the same pointer twice; realloc-as-free is being double-counted by the checker.

rationale: The allocation from avpriv_float_dsp_alloc is stored in the long-lived struct member s->fdsp (AACEncContext), transferring ownership to the codec context. Cleanup is not expected in dsp_init; it occurs in the encoder's close/free routine (e.g. aac_encode_end via av_freep(&s->fdsp)). The early returns on av_tx_init failure leave fdsp owned by s and freed later, so there is no leak on these paths. The checker is intra-procedural and misses the cross-function ownership.

The allocations at line 1143-1144 are stored into AACEncContext struct fields (s->buffer.samples and s->cpe), not local variables. Ownership is transferred to the codec context, which FFmpeg frees in the encoder's close callback (aac_encode_end via ff_af_queue/av_freep on s->buffer.samples and s->cpe). On the ENOMEM error path the codec end function still runs and releases partial allocations. The slice shows ownership transfer, not a leak; the free simply lives outside the provided slice.

The allocations at line 1143-1144 are stored into AACEncContext members (s->buffer.samples and s->cpe), not local variables. Ownership is transferred to the long-lived encoder context, which FFmpeg frees in its encoder close/uninit callback (aac_encode_end via av_freep on s->buffer.samples and s->cpe). On the failure path the function returns AVERROR(ENOMEM) and the context teardown still releases any partially-allocated buffers, so no leak occurs on the reported path.

avpriv_float_dsp_alloc result is stored in the persistent context field s->fdsp, transferring ownership to the AACEncContext. It is not a leak within dsp_init; the encoder's cleanup routine (aac_encode_end) is responsible for freeing s->fdsp via av_freep. The checker only sees no free in local scope.

The allocations at line 1143 are assigned to persistent context struct fields (s->buffer.samples and s->cpe), not local variables. Ownership is transferred to the AACEncContext, whose lifetime extends beyond alloc_buffers. In FFmpeg these buffers are released in the encoder close callback (aac_encode_end), so they are not leaked at function scope. The NeverFree report reflects the analyzer not tracing the free in the separate close routine.

The allocations at line 1144 (FF_ALLOCZ_TYPED_ARRAY for s->buffer.samples and s->cpe) write into members of the AACEncContext struct, not local variables. Ownership is transferred to the long-lived encoder context, which FFmpeg frees in the codec's close/end callback (aac_encode_end via av_freep on s->cpe, s->buffer.samples, etc.). The function returning AVERROR(ENOMEM) on partial failure is also handled by the normal codec cleanup path. The analyzer's NeverFree finding reflects only the local scope of alloc_buffers and misses the deferred free, making this a false positive.

The av_calloc result at line 371 is stored in pctx->ch, where pctx is ctx->model_priv_data. Ownership is transferred to the persistent FFPsyContext, which is released by the corresponding psy_3gpp_end cleanup function (not shown in slice). On the local failure path (line 372-374) the previously allocated model_priv_data is freed. This is intra-function ownership transfer to a long-lived context, not a leak.

The allocation at line 371 is stored into pctx->ch, where pctx is ctx->model_priv_data, a persistent context field. Ownership is transferred to the long-lived FFPsyContext, which is released by the corresponding psy_3gpp_end/close routine (standard FFmpeg init/uninit pairing). On the failure branch (lines 372-375) the prior allocation model_priv_data is freed before returning. The NeverFree checker only sees this init function and cannot observe the matching teardown, so the reported never-free is a false positive.

The allocation at line 72 is stored in the persistent struct field s->frame (avctx->priv_data), not in a local variable. Ownership is transferred to the codec context, which in FFmpeg is released by the decoder's close callback (aasc_decode_end via av_frame_free). The init function correctly handles the only local failure path (lines 73-74). The analyzer flags NeverFree because it does not see the close function, but the resource lifetime is tied to the context, not this function.
